1: The superscription identifies this as 'A Shiggaion of David, sung to the LORD concerning Cush the Benjaminite.' Shiggaion is a rare term (also in Habakkuk 3:1); possibly a type of wandering or passionate lament, or a musical classification. The Benjaminite connection locates Cush within Saul's tribal network, reflecting the ongoing political rivalry between the Benjaminite-Saulide faction and the Davidic-Judahite claim to kingship. The Psalm is structured as a legal procedure: complaint (Psalms 7:1-2) → oath of innocence (Psalms 7:3-5) → appeal to divine judge (Psalms 7:6-9) → confidence in verdict (Psalms 7:10-17). It is one of the most formally legal of all the Psalms.
2: 'Lest they tear my soul apart like a lion, mauling it with no one to rescue' — the lion (aryeh) as the all-devouring enemy appears throughout ANE lament literature and was a live threat in ancient Israel (Samson, David, and Daniel all encounter lions; 1 Kings 13:24 records a lion killing a disobedient prophet). The image combines visceral physical danger with social helplessness: no one to rescue. The patron-client system in ancient Israel meant that a person without a powerful patron was genuinely vulnerable; the psalmist has been effectively abandoned by human allies and appealed to YHWH as the only remaining patron.
3: 'O LORD my God, if I have done this — if there is guilt on my hands' — the conditional self-curse or oath of innocence. This ANE legal procedure is documented in texts from Nuzi (15th c. BCE), the Hammurabi Code (18th c. BCE), and Hittite treaty law: when evidence was insufficient to determine guilt, a defendant could swear a conditional oath calling divine punishment down on himself if he were lying. The divine court would then render judgment through the petitioner's subsequent fate. By swearing 'if I am guilty, let my enemy destroy me,' the psalmist voluntarily submits to divine adjudication rather than human testimony.
4: 'If I have repaid evil to one who was at peace with me, or stripped my enemy without cause' — the specific charges implied by these conditions: repaying peace with aggression, or despoiling an enemy without legal grounds. In the honor-shame culture of ancient Israel, unprovoked aggression against someone 'at peace with you' (shelmi, one in a covenant of non-aggression with you) was a serious violation of social covenant. Cush the Benjaminite has apparently accused David of exactly this kind of treacherous behavior toward a former ally.
5: 'Then let the enemy pursue and overtake me, trample my life into the ground, and lay my honor in the dust' — the self-curse clause of the oath of innocence. By calling down these consequences on himself if guilty, the psalmist makes his innocence claim the most costly possible: he stakes his life and honor on the truth of his denial. The language ('lay my honor in the dust') uses the vocabulary of the utterly shamed: dust was the posture of the defeated, the enslaved, and the dead. The oath's extremity demonstrates the seriousness with which the psalmist takes both the charge and the truth of his denial.
6: 'Rise up, O LORD, in your anger; rouse yourself against the rage of my enemies. Bestir yourself — you have decreed judgment!' — the divine warrior invocation echoes Numbers 10:35 (the ark's battle cry). 'You have decreed judgment' — the psalmist claims that YHWH has already issued a verdict in the heavenly court; the petition is not for a new judgment but for the implementation of an existing one. This assumes the theological framework of the divine assembly (cf. Ps 82; Job 1-2; 1 Kings 22:19-22) where YHWH presides over the council of divine beings and issues cosmic decrees.
7: 'Let the assembly of peoples gather around you, and take your seat on high above them' — the cosmic court: YHWH as judge of all nations, not merely Israel's patron-deity. The claim that YHWH exercises judicial sovereignty over all peoples was theologically distinctive in the ancient Near East where deities had national jurisdictions (Chemosh for Moab, Baal for Canaan, Marduk for Babylon). The psalmist's appeal to YHWH to judge universally is simultaneously a claim about the universal scope of YHWH's authority and a statement that the verdict against Cush will be rendered by the highest possible court.
8: 'Vindicate me, O LORD, according to my righteousness and the integrity that is in me' — the 'righteousness' (tsidqi) claimed is not moral perfection but covenant loyalty and specifically innocence of the particular charge being brought. ANE legal procedure distinguished between a person's overall moral standing and their innocence of a specific charge; the psalmist does not claim sinlessness but claims innocence of what Cush alleged. The appeal to 'integrity' (tummi, completeness/wholeness) is the claim that the psalmist's inner life matches his outer conduct — no hidden guilt corresponding to the charge.
9: 'Let the wickedness of the wicked come to an end and establish the righteous, for the righteous God searches hearts and minds' — the divine capacity for omniscient judgment that no human court possesses. The heart (lev) was the seat of thought and will; the kidneys (klayot) were understood as the seat of deep emotion and motivation. Together they represent the complete inner life — hidden from human inspection but transparent to YHWH. The divine court has access to what human courts cannot reach: the actual motivations behind the action. This epistemic advantage is the psalmist's ground for confidence in appealing to YHWH over human adjudication.
10: 'My shield is God, the Most High (El Elyon), who saves those who are upright in heart' — El Elyon (Most High God) is the title first used by Melchizedek, priest-king of Salem (Genesis 14:18-22), which Abraham identified as identical with YHWH. The title carries the connotation of cosmic sovereignty above all other divine beings and earthly rulers — the Most High of the divine assembly (cf. Deuteronomy 32:8-9). Invoking YHWH as El Elyon is appealing to the supreme cosmic authority, whose jurisdiction admits no superior court, no overruling authority.
11: 'God is a righteous judge, a God who expresses indignation every day' — the continuous moral assessment: YHWH's justice is not occasional but daily and constant. This counters the practical atheism that assumed YHWH was inattentive to daily human conduct (Psalms 10:11: 'He says in his heart, God has forgotten, he has hidden his face, he will never see it'). The daily expression of divine indignation at wickedness means that no act of injustice escapes the divine court's notice, even when human courts fail or are manipulated.
12: 'If a man does not repent, God will sharpen his sword; he has bent his bow and strung it' — the conditional structure preserves the sinner's freedom: the sword is sharp but not yet wielded; repentance (shuva, again) is still the open door. This is the same mercy-structure underlying the prophetic warnings: the judgment is announced so that repentance may forestall it (cf. Jeremiah 18:7-10; Jonah 3:10). The divine warrior's weapon-preparation is not inevitable execution but the penultimate warning before judgment falls.
13: 'He has prepared deadly weapons against the persecutors; his arrows will be fiery shafts' — reshef was the Canaanite deity of plague and burning fever (appearing in Canaanite texts from Ugarit as the god who shoots arrows of disease). Here reshef is entirely demythologized: not an autonomous deity of destruction but merely one of YHWH's weapons — the divine quiver contains plagues and fire as instruments of covenant judgment. The polemical move is characteristic: what surrounding cultures attributed to divine beings the Psalter attributes to YHWH's sovereign deployment.
14: 'The wicked person conceives evil, becomes pregnant with mischief, and gives birth to deception' — the full birth metaphor for sin's development in three stages (conception → pregnancy → delivery). James 1:15 deliberately picks up this exact imagery: 'desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death.' The biological metaphor communicates that sin has an internal developmental logic: it does not remain static but grows toward expression. The ancient wisdom tradition across Egypt (Instruction of Amenemope), Babylon (wisdom proverbs), and Israel used similar developmental language for moral corruption.
15: 'He dug a pit, made it deep, and fell into the very pit he made' — the pit-boomerang principle appears across ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature: the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope warns that the one who sets a trap for others falls in himself; Babylonian proverbs similarly describe the failure of schemes that rebound on the schemer. The pit (bor) in Israel's legal landscape was the covered trap for catching wild animals, adapted here as a metaphor for the legal or social trap set for the psalmist. The principle of poetic justice — that wrongdoing is structurally self-defeating — was a cross-cultural wisdom conviction.
16: 'His mischief comes back on his own head; his violence falls down on his own skull' — the lex talionis (the principle that punishment mirrors the crime) applied at the cosmic level. Violence (chamas) is the term for lawless force and the breach of social order (Habakkuk 1:2-3; Genesis 6:11 — chamas was the corruption that provoked the Flood). That violence returns to the violent person's own skull (qodqod) completes the boomerang: the force directed outward curves back with full effect. The theological claim is that the moral order of creation is structured so that injustice eventually destroys its own practitioners.
17: 'I will give thanks to the LORD for his righteousness, and sing praise to the name of the LORD Most High' — the Psalm that opened with legal complaint and oath of innocence ends in anticipated thanksgiving. The transition from petition to praise — before the verdict is publicly rendered — is the pattern of covenant trust: the psalmist acts on the certainty of YHWH's justice before seeing its outcome. The 'name' (shem) of YHWH encapsulates the totality of revealed divine character; praise of the Name is praise of everything YHWH has made himself known to be through covenant history, including the justice the Psalm has invoked.
